questions.q: In a rectangular current loop in a magnetic field, why do the two active sides experience forces in opposite directions?
options: The two sides carry current in opposite directions around the loop
The field is stronger on one side than the other
The two sides are made of different materials
One side is closer to the north pole
questions.explanation: It is one continuous wire, so the current runs up one active side and down the opposite side — antiparallel. With the same external field but opposite currents, the motor-effect forces are equal in size but opposite in direction (one out of the page, one into the page).

questions.q: Two equal, opposite, parallel forces whose lines of action are offset from the axis form a:
options: Net translational force
Couple, which produces rotation without translation
Centripetal force
Balanced pair with no effect
questions.explanation: Such an arrangement is a couple. It produces a net turning effect (torque) but zero net translational force, so the loop spins on its axle rather than being pushed bodily across the field.

questions.q: As the coil rotates, why does the torque change even though the force on the active sides stays constant?
options: The force weakens as the coil turns
The current decreases as the coil turns
The perpendicular distance (lever arm) from the axis to the lines of action changes
The field reverses direction
questions.explanation: The active sides stay perpendicular to the field, so the force stays constant at \(BIL\). What changes is the lever arm — the perpendicular distance from the axis to the lines of action — so the torque varies purely because of geometry.

questions.q: At what orientation is the torque on a current loop a maximum?
options: When the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field
When the plane of the coil is parallel to the field
When the current is zero
When the coil is stationary
questions.explanation: When the plane of the coil is parallel to the field, the two forces act at the maximum perpendicular distance from the axis, giving maximum lever arm and maximum torque (\(\cos 0^\circ = 1\)).

questions.q: When the plane of the coil is perpendicular to the field, the torque is zero. Why?
options: The forces on the sides become zero
The current stops flowing
The forces act along the axis of rotation, so the perpendicular distance is zero
The field becomes zero at that orientation
questions.explanation: The forces are still full strength (\(BIL\)), but their lines of action pass through the axis, so the perpendicular distance — and hence the turning effect — is zero. Saying the torque is zero "because there is no force" is a marked error.

questions.q: The torque on a coil is \(\tau = nBIA\cos\theta\) (with \(\theta\) the angle between the field and the plane of the coil). The SI unit of torque is:
options: The joule (J)
The newton metre (N m)
The watt (W)
The tesla (T)
questions.explanation: Torque is a force times a perpendicular distance, so its unit is the newton metre. Although a newton metre is dimensionally the same as a joule, torque is not energy and writing joules here is a marked error.

questions.q: A coil of 50 turns, area \(4\times10^{-3}\ \mathrm{m^2}\), carries 2 A in a 0.25 T field. What is the maximum torque?
The formula sheet writes torque as \(\tau = nBIA\sin\theta\), but this episode uses \(\cos\theta\). Why are both correct?
options: They are different physics for different motors
The two define \(\theta\) from different references (plane vs normal), and \(\cos\) of one equals \(\sin\) of the other
One is for AC and one for DC
The sine form ignores the number of turns
questions.explanation: With \(\theta\) measured to the plane of the coil you use cosine; with \(\theta\) measured to the normal you use sine. The two angles are complementary, so \(\cos\) of one equals \(\sin\) of the other — identical physics. The trap is using the wrong function for the angle the question gives.

questions.q: Which set of changes would all increase the torque on a current loop?
options: Fewer turns, weaker field, smaller current, smaller area
More turns, stronger field, larger current, larger area
More turns, weaker field, smaller current, larger area
Fewer turns, stronger field, larger current, smaller area
questions.explanation: From \(\tau = nBIA\cos\theta\), torque is directly proportional to \(n\), \(B\), \(I\) and \(A\). Increasing any of them increases the torque, so increasing all four does. The four torque-boosters are read straight off the formula.

questions.q: Why does a bare current loop fail to spin continuously in one direction?
options: The torque is constant, so the loop never starts moving
Its momentum carries it past the zero-torque position, after which the unchanged forces push it back, so it oscillates
The forces always point along the axis
The loop overheats and stops
questions.explanation: A bare loop reaches the zero-torque (perpendicular) position, but inertia carries it past. Past that point the forces (unchanged in direction) produce a torque the opposite way, so the loop oscillates rather than rotating. A split-ring commutator fixes this by reversing the current at the dead point.
